A History of Mind

Renaissance Early Modern

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Jean Buridan

c. 1300 – c. 1358

Medieval nominalism, cognition, and the mechanics of assent and will.

John Buridan did not write a modern “philosophy of mind” treatise. What we can extract is a systematic doctrine from his commentaries on Aristotle’s De anima, his logic in the Summulae de dialectica, his treatments of natural philosophy and metaphysics, and his moral psychology. The result is not a Cartesian theory of an inner private ego, nor a reductive materialism, nor a merely theological psychology. It is better described as an empiricist, nominalist, act-centered, hylomorphic philosophy of mind, whose most delicate point is the human intellect: embodied and causally integrated with sensory life, yet, according to the position Buridan treats as the Catholic truth, immaterial, created, plural in each person, and contingently preserved after death by God.

The central interpretive problem is that Buridan often distinguishes what can be established by natural reason from what must be received from faith. This distinction should not be read as indifference to theology. Buridan’s remarks about God, angels, separate substances, divine omnipotence, faith, and immortality are often doing real philosophical work: they mark the limits of natural psychology, identify higher possible forms of mind, and clarify the modal status of the human intellect. Buridan was a Paris arts master, not a theologian, and Stanford’s account rightly stresses that he framed philosophy as inquiry from what is evident to sense and intellect, while theology proceeds from revealed articles; but the same source also notes that both disciplines can consider God and divine matters, though from different principles.

1. The textual and scholarly situation

Buridan’s psychology is principally found in his question-commentary on Aristotle’s De anima. The modern textual situation is unusually important here. The complete Latin text and English translation of Buridan’s Questions on Aristotle’s De anima appeared in 2023, edited and translated by Gyula Klima, Peter G. Sobol, Martin J. Hartman, and Jack Zupko; a 2025 NDPR review notes that the commentary was completed after 1347 and probably before 1361, and that it is not merely a paraphrase of Aristotle but an original question-commentary on life, perception, cognition, and thought.

There are also multiple versions or redactions of Buridan’s De anima commentary. Chiara Beneduce emphasizes that Buridan appears to have lectured on De anima more than once, that manuscripts preserve a tertia sive ultima lectura as well as an earlier version, and that the early modern Lokert edition complicates the picture further. This matters because some debates about Buridan’s real position on the intellect turn partly on which text or passage is being emphasized.

The most disputed interpretive issue is whether Buridan’s treatment of the human intellect is basically materialist, covertly Alexandrist, or genuinely aligned with the position of faith that the human intellect is an immaterial, immortal substantial form inhering in the human body. Olaf Pluta has argued for a more materialist or Alexandrist reading; Beneduce, developing a position closer to Jack Zupko’s, argues that Buridan does not finally endorse Alexandrian materialism and that his appeal to faith is not a mere institutional formality.

The reconstruction below follows the latter line, but with an important qualification: Buridan deliberately shows that unaided natural reason has powerful motives for a more materialist account. So the mature Buridanian philosophy of mind contains a tension rather than a simple dogma. Natural psychology presses toward embodied empiricism; faith and metaphysical-theological principles secure the immaterial and post-mortem status of the human intellect.

2. Psychology as the science of the soul, powers, and operations

For Buridan, the science corresponding to Aristotle’s De anima is the science of the soul, its powers, and its characteristic functions. Susan Brower-Toland’s study of Buridan’s self-knowledge doctrine makes this explicit: the proper subject of psychology is not simply the body, nor simply the soul-body composite, but the soul and its powers or functions insofar as they belong to the soul.

This does not make psychology an introspective science of a disembodied subject. Buridan’s natural philosopher studies the soul through its operations in living bodies. Zupko’s Stanford entry stresses that Buridan refuses, within natural philosophy, to speculate freely about the soul’s inner essence; the natural philosopher defines the soul by its proper matter, namely the physical organic body, and ultimate questions about the soul’s nature are partly left to theology.

That methodological restraint is one of the keys to Buridan. He is not saying the soul has no deeper nature. He is saying that natural psychology has its own evidential discipline. It starts from living operations: nutrition, sensation, memory, imagination, intellectual understanding, volition, and action. The mind is therefore approached through what it does, not through an immediate metaphysical inspection of what it is.

This is why Buridan’s philosophy of mind is act-centered. A mind is not primarily a container of inner objects. It is a principle of operations: sensing, judging, remembering, imagining, understanding, assenting, doubting, willing, suspending, and choosing. His logic then extends this act-centered picture by treating concepts and mental propositions as acts or structures of cognition.

3. The unity of the soul and the status of powers

Buridan holds that in a human being there is one soul, not a really distinct vegetative soul, sensitive soul, and intellective soul layered on top of one another. Brower-Toland notes that Buridan does not posit an ontological distinction among vegetative, sensitive, and intellective souls in human beings; “intellect” names the human soul considered as the principle or power of rational thought.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Buridan does not divide the human subject into separate psychological substances. Nutrition, sensation, imagination, and intellection are rooted in one soul, though described under different concepts according to different operations. Sylvain Roudaut’s 2024 study of Buridan and Oresme on the soul’s presence in the body explains that Buridan treats powers, in the principal sense, as identical with the soul and only rationally distinct from it; in a looser sense, powers can be distinguished by the diverse operations a subject performs.

This is a nominalist and parsimonious treatment of faculties. Buridan does not want to multiply real entities called “faculties” whenever we can distinguish operations. The intellect, will, memory, senses, and vegetative powers are not all separate little substances inside the person. They are ways in which the same living soul is describable as a principle of different acts.

At the same time, Buridan does not collapse psychology into physiology. Roudaut shows that Buridan’s account of the soul’s presence in the body tends to preserve a stronger distinction between soul and body than Nicole Oresme’s, especially in non-human animals. Buridan’s hylomorphism is not Cartesian substance dualism, but it is not reductive materialism either.

A concise formulation would be: Buridan is ontologically parsimonious about powers, but not eliminativist about soul. He reduces faculties to conceptual distinctions grounded in operations, but he does not reduce the soul itself to a mere aggregate of bodily processes.

4. The three famous positions on the human intellect

Buridan’s central discussion of the human intellect in De anima III, questions 3–6, is organized around three famous opinions.

The first is associated with Alexander of Aphrodisias. On this view, the human intellect is material, generable and corruptible, extended with matter, and does not remain after death. The second is associated with Averroes. On this view, the intellect is immaterial, incorruptible, not educed from matter, not extended, but also one for all human beings and not a form inhering in individual bodies. The third is the position of Catholic faith: the human intellect is a substantial form inhering in the human body, not educed from matter, not extended, not naturally generated or corrupted, created anew, plural in different persons, and preserved after death unless God annihilates it by absolute power.

This tripartite taxonomy is not decorative. It maps the possible answers to five linked questions: whether the intellect inheres in the body, whether it is educed from matter, whether it is extended, whether it is naturally generated and corrupted, and whether there is one intellect for all or many intellects corresponding to many persons. Alain de Libera’s French analysis of “formes assistantes” and “formes inhérentes” emphasizes exactly this structure: Buridan’s discussion contrasts an Averroist “assisting” or non-inhering separate form with the faith-position according to which the intellect is an inhering substantial form.

The position Buridan treats as true is a form of immanent dualism. It is dualist because the human intellect is immaterial, not naturally generated from matter, not spatially extended, and not naturally corruptible. It is immanent because this intellect is not an external shared mind or separate assistant intelligence; it inheres in the individual human body as the substantial form by which a human being is human. Beneduce summarizes the faith-position as combining the plurality and inherence denied by Averroes with the immateriality and immortality denied by Alexander.

This is one of the most important points for extracting a Buridanian philosophy of mind. Buridan does not choose between “embodied mind” and “immaterial intellect” by rejecting one side. He tries to hold both. The human intellect is embodied as the form of a particular human body; it is not embodied as an extended material form generated by bodily mixture. It is individuated in persons; it is not a shared cosmic intellect. It is not accessible to the senses; yet all ordinary human intellectual life depends on sensory cognition and phantasms.

5. The soul-body relation: hylomorphism, not Cartesian dualism

Buridan’s view should not be read backward through Descartes. He does not posit a complete thinking substance accidentally attached to a body-machine. The soul is the form of a living body. The human being is not a mind plus a body, but an ensouled body whose soul is the principle of life and thought.

Yet Buridan’s position is also not Aristotelianism in the simplest naturalistic form. The human intellective soul is not naturally generated by matter and does not perish naturally with bodily corruption. The body is essential to ordinary human cognition, but the intellect’s own mode of being is not exhausted by bodily extension.

Buridan’s position is therefore structurally triadic:

First, for natural psychology, the soul is known through embodied operations.

Second, for metaphysics of the human soul, the human intellective soul is an inhering substantial form that makes the human being human.

Third, for theological modal status, the human intellect is created and preserved by God; its post-mortem continuation is not a brute natural necessity independent of divine will.

Beneduce’s reading is especially helpful here because she argues that Buridan does not simply tack faith onto an otherwise materialist psychology. Rather, Buridan allows faith to provide principles that natural reason alone could not fully establish; on this basis, the intellect’s immortality is contingent on God’s will, not a self-grounded necessary eternity.

This gives us a philosophically serious role for God in Buridan’s philosophy of mind. God is not an irrelevant theological add-on. God’s creative and annihilative power determines the modal profile of the human intellect: the intellect is not naturally corrupted, but neither is it absolutely unannihilable. It is sempiternal a parte post by divine will, not metaphysically independent of God.

6. Sensation: species, attribution, and the soul’s causal role

Buridan’s account of sense perception is one of the most philosophically interesting parts of his psychology. The 2025 NDPR review of the 2023 edition explains that Buridan distinguishes the reception of sensible species in organs from cognition proper. Sensible species can be received in air, in organs, and even in a corpse; therefore, reception alone is not cognition. Perception requires an attribution or judgment that relates the sensible content to the external object or environment.

This is an important anti-passivist point. To perceive is not merely to have a sensory organ modified. Perception involves an act of the soul. The sensory system receives species or impressions, but cognition occurs when the living soul attributes or takes something as being thus-and-so in the world.

The same NDPR review notes that Buridan treats sensation as a sui generis quality and gives the soul both a subject-role and an efficient-causal role in sensation. Physiological processing is necessary, but the soul is not eliminated in favor of physiology.

In modern terms, Buridan is not a simple sense-data theorist. He is closer to an act theory of perception with representational and judgmental structure. Sensation involves bodily reception, but perception proper includes an intentional directedness toward the world.

The corpse example is especially revealing. If a corpse’s eye or organ can receive a sensible form but cannot perceive, then perception is not identical with the physical reception of stimulus information. Buridan’s philosophy of mind therefore has room for a real distinction between sensory information-bearing processes and living conscious perception.

7. Internal sense, phantasms, and the dependence of intellect on sense

Buridan is a robust empiricist about human cognition. Brower-Toland states that, for Buridan, all human knowledge begins in the senses or is derived from sense experience. The intellect’s concepts of intellect and intellective states themselves must somehow be grounded in experience, even though the intellect is not externally sensed.

Gyula Klima’s study of Buridan’s acquisition of simple substantial concepts makes the empiricist thesis even sharper: Buridan’s first conclusion is that the first intellectual cognition is obtained from sensory cognition, and that every intellectual cognition is obtained directly or indirectly from sensory cognition.

This empiricism is mediated by phantasms. Brower-Toland notes that raw data from external and internal senses are prerequisites for intelligible species, and that Buridan is inclined to identify intelligible species with phantasms or acts of the cogitative power.

This gives us another major thesis: Buridan’s intellect is not born furnished with innate concepts. Nor does it operate in ordinary human life independently of sensory images and internal sensory acts. Human understanding requires attention to phantasms. Yet this dependence does not make intellectual cognition identical to sense cognition. The intellect can grasp universals, substances, and propositions in ways that sensory organs alone cannot.

Buridan’s empiricism is therefore neither crude sensationalism nor skeptical phenomenalism. It is a layered empiricism: sensation supplies the basis; internal sense and phantasms mediate; intellect forms concepts, judgments, and scientific knowledge.

8. The acquisition of substantial concepts

A major difficulty for empiricism is how we acquire concepts of substances if the senses seem to give us only qualities, accidents, and appearances. Buridan’s answer is subtle. Klima argues that Buridan can be a thoroughgoing empiricist without collapsing into what Klima calls a kind of “medieval Humeanism” or Autrecourt-style skepticism.

Buridan holds that one cognition can arise from another. Sensory cognition of accidents can lead to intellectual cognition of substances, not merely by formal inference, but through the way higher cognitive powers take lower cognitive acts as objects. The intellect can be moved by phantasms, and through the cognition of accidents one can arrive at cognition of substances.

This is crucial for philosophy of mind because it prevents Buridan’s empiricism from becoming a theory of mere phenomenal surfaces. The mind does not simply collect patches of color, sound, taste, and touch. It can form concepts of enduring things. It can classify, recognize, and reason about substances.

Klima’s Spanish-language presentation of the same issue stresses that the problem is not trivial: scientific knowledge requires universal and necessary generalizations and substantial concepts, yet the mind begins without content and acquires concepts from experience. Buridan’s theory is important precisely because it tries to explain how an empiricist can acquire substantial concepts without reducing them to bundles of sensible qualities.

A modern comparison may help, though it is only heuristic. Buridan is trying to explain how an empiricist can be a realist about objects. He thinks perceptual experience gives us access to the world in a structured way sufficient for the intellect to form concepts of substances, not just concepts of sensory features.

9. Singular cognition and universality

Buridan does not think cognition begins with abstract universals. The Stanford entry on medieval mental representation reports that, for Buridan, first cognition or conception is singular. Human beings can have vague and determinate singular concepts, such as “this thing,” “this body,” “this animal,” “this donkey,” or “Brunellus.”

This matters because Buridan’s philosophy of mind does not begin from universal concepts floating free of perception. The intellect’s conceptual life is rooted in encounters with singular things. Universal cognition is built from a cognitive basis in singular experience.

But Buridan also thinks the intellect can cognize universally. This is one of the differences between intellectual cognition and mere sensory reception. The mind can move from this animal to animal, from this human being to human being, and from perceived cases to universal or scientific propositions.

The resulting picture is neither pure nominalist atomism nor Platonic realism. Concepts are acts of individual minds, but they can function universally. Universality is not a separately existing universal thing; it is a mode of signifying or conceiving.

10. Concepts as mental acts and the semantic primacy of mental language

Buridan’s logic is indispensable for his philosophy of mind. The Summulae de dialectica is not merely a logic textbook in the narrow modern sense; Gyula Klima’s introduction notes that it contains a systematic nominalist logic with implications for philosophy of science, language, and mind.

In Buridan’s semantic theory, written and spoken terms are subordinated to concepts or mental acts. Klima explains that mental propositions are singular acts of individual human minds and are the primary bearers of truth and falsity; spoken and written propositions are meaningful through their association with those cognitive acts.

This yields a strong philosophy-of-mind thesis: thought has propositional structure before public language expresses it. Spoken language signifies by being connected to mental language. But this does not mean Buridan is a psychologistic subjectivist. Mental acts are directed toward things, and logic studies the formal and semantic structures by which terms and propositions supposit, signify, and yield valid inference.

Buridan’s “mental language” is not exactly the same as a modern innate Mentalese. Klima stresses that Buridan allows different speakers and languages to develop different conceptual apparatuses; mental language is not a single uniform ideal language shared identically by all minds.

So Buridan combines semantic mentalism with conceptual historicity and variability. Words depend on concepts, but concepts are not a fixed divine dictionary implanted in every human mind. Human conceptual schemes develop through experience, use, instruction, and intellectual acts.

11. Signification, supposition, and intentionality

Buridan’s terminist logic distinguishes signification from supposition. Zupko’s Stanford entry explains that signification concerns making something known to the mind, while supposition is contextual reference, roughly comparable to reference in a proposition.

This has direct relevance for philosophy of mind. Buridan can distinguish the psychological act by which a concept makes something understood from the logical role by which a term stands for something in a propositional context. Mental content is not a mysterious object inserted into the mind. It is an act with semantic function.

His theory also helps with intentional contexts. Klima notes Buridan’s doctrine of appellatio rationis, the “appellation of concepts,” which became influential for the analysis of intentional contexts.

This is where Buridan’s philosophy of mind intersects with philosophy of language in a particularly modern-looking way. Believing, knowing, doubting, promising, fearing, and desiring often involve how things are conceived, not merely which external objects exist. Buridan’s logical tools allow him to analyze the difference between reference to things and appeal to concepts or modes of conceiving.

12. Mental complexity without ontological inflation

Buridan’s nominalism is not a flattening of mental life. He can acknowledge complex thoughts while refusing to multiply corresponding entities in the world.

Klima gives a useful example involving propositions such as “God is God” and “God is not God.” These may concern the same simple external entity, God, but they are not the same apud mentem, that is, not the same in the mind; the difference is supplied by the mental proposition and its copulative structure rather than by multiplying divine or extramental entities.

This is philosophically significant. Buridan separates mental or semantic complexity from extramental ontological complexity. A complex thought need not correspond to a complex object. This allows him to preserve a sparse ontology while giving a rich account of propositional content.

It also shows why theological examples are not distractions. Propositions about God help Buridan clarify the relation between thought, language, and reality. The simplicity of God becomes a test case for whether semantic complexity requires ontological complexity. Buridan’s answer is no: the mind can form distinct propositions concerning the same simple divine reality.

13. Self-knowledge: neither pure introspection nor mere behaviorism

Buridan’s account of self-knowledge is one of the most sophisticated parts of his philosophy of mind. The core difficulty is clear: if all human knowledge begins from sense, and the intellect cannot be externally sensed, how can the intellect know itself?

Brower-Toland emphasizes that Buridan’s De anima III, q. 9 asks whether the human intellect can intellectually cognize itself. The issue is not merely whether an individual occasionally notices a thought, but how we can possess a general concept of intellect at all.

Buridan rejects a simple model of immediate self-transparent intellectual essence. The human intellect does not begin by gazing directly at its own essence. Instead, our concept of intellect is grounded in awareness of our intellective acts: universal cognition, reasoning, judging, and other rational operations.

Brower-Toland interprets Buridan as appealing to a kind of self-directed experience or awareness of our own intellective states and rational activities. She argues that this experience is nonconceptual and nondiscursive at its base, comparable in some respects to what contemporary philosophers call phenomenal consciousness, though Buridan himself of course does not use that terminology.

The resulting theory is balanced. Buridan is not a behaviorist: self-knowledge is grounded in lived awareness of one’s own cognitive activity. But he is also not a Cartesian: the intellect does not have immediate, complete, innate knowledge of its own essence. Self-knowledge is mediated by operations, reflection, and conceptualization.

A Buridanian theory of self-knowledge would therefore say: I know mind by experiencing myself thinking, reasoning, and understanding; from those acts I form the concept of intellect. The act is epistemically prior to the essence.

14. Animal minds and non-linguistic cognition

Buridan’s philosophy of mind is not restricted to human beings. Olaf Pluta’s study of mental representation in animals and humans argues that late medieval discussions, including Buridan’s, should not assume a necessary connection between language and thought.

Buridan grants remarkably sophisticated cognition to animals. Pluta reports that Buridan clearly affirms that animals, including dogs, can refer to things universally: a dog can mentally represent particular water as falling under a universal category such as water.

Pluta further notes that Buridan allows higher animals to have sensation, memory, experience, and even something like universal knowledge based on induction. In practical examples, such as a dog avoiding a pit on the way home and taking another route, Buridan attributes a kind of logical reasoning or syllogizing to the animal, though not as subtly or completely as in humans.

This is a major point. Buridan is not a crude linguistic intellectualist for whom only speakers think. Non-human animals can represent, remember, categorize, learn, and reason practically. The difference between human and animal minds is not the difference between thought and no thought; it is a graded difference involving intellective universality, scientific reasoning, linguistic articulation, and the special metaphysical status of the human intellective soul.

This also recontextualizes the famous “Buridan’s ass” story. Stanford notes that the example of Buridan’s ass is not actually found in Buridan’s writings and is probably a parody of his view of free choice.

15. Will, freedom, and practical reason

Buridan’s moral psychology is intellectualist, but not simplistic. Stanford’s account of Buridan’s ethics emphasizes his concern with the relation between will and intellect, freedom, weakness of will, and practical reason. The will can suspend or defer choice when the intellect does not judge clearly which option is better, but even that suspension is made under an intellectual presentation of some good.

This means the will is not an arbitrary power of unreasoned self-determination. The will depends on how the intellect represents goods. Buridan’s account preserves freedom by allowing suspension, reconsideration, and non-immediate choice, but it does not make willing independent of cognition.

The same pattern appears in discussions of faith and assent. A French Sorbonne study of voluntary certitude notes that Buridan, as an arts master, did not produce a full theological theory of fides, but that belief, assent, fallibility, and firmness of assent are central to his broader theory.

That study also characterizes Buridan’s position as intellectualist: the will’s role in faith is limited to orienting or suspending the intellect, rather than absolutely determining belief by sheer volition.

So Buridan’s philosophy of agency is cognitive at its core. Desire and will respond to goods as represented. Freedom is not irrational spontaneity; it is the capacity of a rational agent to deliberate, suspend, and act under intellectual apprehension of the good.

16. Happiness and the mind’s highest object

Buridan’s account of happiness also belongs to philosophy of mind. Stanford reports that, for Buridan, happiness is an intellectual act, specifically a perfect apprehension of God.

This is not a marginal pious flourish. It tells us what Buridan thinks the highest perfection of mind is. The human mind is not fulfilled merely by sensory pleasure, practical success, or even ordinary scientific cognition. Its highest act is intellectual apprehension of the highest object.

This also gives God another central role in Buridan’s philosophy of mind. God is not only the creator and possible annihilator of the intellect; God is also the supreme intelligible object toward which perfected intellectual life is ordered.

In modern terms, Buridan’s philosophy of mind is teleological. Human cognition is not merely information processing. It has an orientation toward truth, science, wisdom, and ultimately God as the highest intelligible good.

17. God, divine mind, and divine omnipotence

Buridan’s comments on God should be treated as philosophically internal to his system. At least four roles of God are relevant.

First, God is the ultimate modal authority over the human intellect. The faith-position says the human intellect is created anew, not naturally generated, and remains after death by divine will, though God could annihilate it by absolute power.

Second, God is the object of perfect intellectual happiness. The human mind’s final perfection is not merely knowing finite nature but apprehending God.

Third, God provides a limiting case for semantics and ontology. Buridan’s use of propositions about God shows that distinct mental propositions can concern the same simple divine reality without requiring corresponding complexity in God.

Fourth, God’s omnipotence sets the boundary between natural certitude and absolute possibility. Stanford notes that Buridan acknowledges divine omnipotence and the possibility that God could deceive, while still maintaining ordinary reliability of perception and induction in the common course of nature.

This is an elegant epistemological balance. Buridan does not infer from divine omnipotence that empirical knowledge is useless. He treats natural science as valid within the common course of nature, while recognizing that absolute divine power is wider than natural regularity.

A Buridanian philosophy of mind therefore includes divine mind and divine power not as optional theology but as a metaphysical horizon. Human cognition is empirical and finite; divine cognition and power set the absolute boundary conditions under which finite minds exist, know, err, and persist.

18. Angels and separate substances

Buridan’s remarks about angels are often methodological. Stanford notes his comment that “great legions of angels” cannot be proved by demonstrations from sense perception.

This should not be read as contempt for angelology. It is a statement about the limits of natural philosophy. Angels, as immaterial minds or separate substances, are not ordinary objects of empirical psychology. Their existence and nature belong more properly to theology unless some philosophical argument can reach them from evident principles.

The 2023 French/Italian publication notice for Jean Celeyrette’s chapter on “Les intelligences séparées dans les commentaires à la Métaphysique de Jean Buridan et Marsile d’Inghien” is a useful reminder that separate intelligences in Buridan’s metaphysics remain a live scholarly topic, especially in non-Anglophone work.

The philosophical significance is this: angels are possible or theologically affirmed minds that test the scope of Buridan’s empiricist psychology. Human psychology is tied to sensation and phantasms. Angelic cognition, if treated theologically, would not be so tied. Thus angels mark the difference between a theory of human mind and a broader theory of intellect as such.

Buridan’s own disciplinary caution prevents him from building a detailed natural science of angelic minds. But precisely that caution is informative. It means his empiricism is a doctrine about human cognition under natural conditions, not a blanket denial that non-sensory intellects could exist.

19. Reason and faith: not two unrelated compartments

The relation between reason and faith in Buridan is one of the most important interpretive questions. Buridan’s arts-faculty context required care. But Beneduce argues that faith is not merely an external institutional constraint in his treatment of the intellect. Rather, Buridan openly adheres to the position she calls immanent dualism and allows faith to help reason reach truths about the intellect that natural reason alone cannot securely attain.

This does not mean Buridan abandons philosophy. It means his complete doctrine of the human mind is layered. Natural reason can investigate cognition, sensation, concept formation, self-knowledge, and psychology. It can also assess arguments for Alexander’s and Averroes’s views. But the final position on the human intellect’s immateriality, inherence, plurality, and post-mortem persistence depends on principles faith supplies.

The strongest reconstruction is therefore not “Buridan the secret materialist” or “Buridan the unphilosophical fideist.” It is Buridan as a disciplined philosophical psychologist whose naturalism is real but not totalizing.

His method is: reason as far as reason can go; mark the point where natural evidence underdetermines the issue; accept revealed truth where faith legitimately supplies what natural reason cannot.

20. A comprehensive Buridanian philosophy of mind: extracted theses

The following theses synthesize the report into a coherent philosophy of mind. 1. Mind is known through operations. We know the soul by what living beings do: sense, remember, imagine, understand, judge, will, and act. Buridan does not begin with a self-transparent Cartesian ego. 2. Psychology is empirical but not reductive. Natural psychology starts from sense-accessible operations and embodied life, yet it does not reduce perception or thought to mere bodily modification. 3. The soul is one; powers are not separate inner substances. Vegetative, sensitive, and intellective “powers” are conceptually distinguished according to operations, but in the principal sense they are not really distinct entities from the soul. 4. Perception is active and attributive. Sensible species received in organs are not yet cognition. Perception requires the soul’s living act of attribution or judgment. 5. All ordinary human intellectual cognition depends on sense. The intellect acquires its concepts from sensory cognition directly or indirectly, through phantasms and internal cognitive acts. 6. Empiricism does not entail phenomenalism. Buridan thinks the intellect can acquire concepts of substances, not merely accidents or appearances. 7. Concepts are mental acts. They are not Platonic objects, nor are they reducible to spoken words. Public language is meaningful through its subordination to mental acts. 8. Mental propositions are primary bearers of truth and falsity. Buridan’s philosophy of mind and philosophy of logic are inseparable. 9. Intentionality is semantically structured. Signification, supposition, appellation, and modes of conceiving explain how thought is directed toward things under descriptions or concepts. 10. Self-knowledge is mediated by cognitive activity. The intellect knows itself through awareness and reflection on its own acts, not by immediate complete intuition of its essence. 11. Animal minds are real and sophisticated. Non-human animals can represent, remember, categorize, learn, and reason practically, though human intellective cognition remains distinctive. 12. The will depends on intellect. Free choice involves judgment, deliberation, and possible suspension, but not arbitrary willing independent of cognition. 13. The human intellect is an immanent immaterial form. Buridan’s final position, on the faith-aligned reading, is that the human intellect inheres in the individual body but is not naturally generated, extended, or corrupted. 14. God is central to the modal status and final orientation of mind. God creates and can annihilate the intellect, is the highest object of intellectual happiness, and provides a limiting case for semantic and metaphysical analysis. 15. Angels and separate substances mark the limits of natural psychology. They are not dismissed as nonsense; they are minds or intellects whose treatment requires theological or metaphysical principles beyond ordinary empirical psychology.

21. Where Buridan sits relative to modern options

Buridan is not a materialist in the modern reductive sense, because he does not reduce soul, sensation, or intellect to bodily mechanism, and his final view of the human intellect is immaterialist.

He is not a Cartesian dualist, because he does not treat the mind as a complete substance externally joined to the body. The soul is the substantial form of the living body.

He is not a simple Aristotelian naturalist, because the human intellect is not merely an organic function naturally generated and corrupted with the body.

He is not a Platonist about concepts, because concepts are acts of individual minds, not independently subsisting universals.

He is not a linguistic behaviorist, because mental language and animal representation precede public speech.

He is closest to a hylomorphic, empiricist, nominalist, intentional-act theory of mind, with a theological-metaphysical doctrine of the human intellect as an immaterial inhering form.

22. The non-Anglophone reception and what it adds

French scholarship is especially helpful for the soul-body issue. Alain de Libera’s analysis of “assisting” and “inhering” forms foregrounds the contrast between an Averroist separate intellect and the faith-position of an intellective form inhering in each human body. This vocabulary clarifies that Buridan’s debate is not simply “materialism versus dualism,” but a more precise dispute over whether intellect is an external assisting substance, an inhering substantial form, or a material generated form.

French work on faith and voluntary certitude also helps prevent a crude reading of Buridan’s “faith” language. The Sorbonne study stresses that Buridan did not write a full theology of faith as an arts master, but that assent, fallibility, and the will’s limited role in orienting or suspending intellect are central to his cognitive psychology.

Spanish-language presentation of Klima’s work usefully highlights the empiricist problem of substantial concepts: how a mind beginning without innate content and relying on experience can nevertheless form universal and scientific concepts of substances. This makes Buridan relevant to later empiricist debates, without anachronistically turning him into Hume or Locke.

Italian and broader European scholarship, including Beneduce’s work, has been especially valuable in resisting a purely secularized reading of Buridan’s psychology. Beneduce’s interpretation insists that Buridan’s faith-position on the intellect is not merely decorative and that faith can function as a genuine explanatory framework within the broader inquiry.

23. Final assessment

A comprehensive Buridanian philosophy of mind should be built around one governing tension: the human mind is empirically dependent on the body for its ordinary operations, yet the human intellect is not, in Buridan’s final faith-aligned position, a naturally generated material power. This tension is not a failure of system; it is the system.

Buridan wants to preserve the full seriousness of embodied cognition: sensation, phantasms, animal reasoning, acquired concepts, fallible assent, and the mind’s dependence on experience. At the same time, he wants to preserve the metaphysical and theological dignity of the human intellect: its individuality, immateriality, inherence as substantial form, post-mortem preservation, and orientation toward God.

The resulting view is unusually rich. It gives us perception without reducing perception to physiology; concepts without Platonic universals; mental language without public-language behaviorism; self-knowledge without Cartesian transparency; animal cognition without human exceptionalism in the crude sense; freedom without arbitrary voluntarism; and theology without dismissing natural reason.

Buridan’s philosophy of mind is therefore best understood as a disciplined medieval theory of embodied intellectual life under divine metaphysical conditions. Its deepest question is not simply “Is the mind material or immaterial?” but rather: how can a finite, embodied, sense-dependent creature think universally, know itself, act freely, form true propositions, share cognition with animals, differ from angels, and be ordered finally toward God?

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
